dv.title: Deployment Visualizer
dv.strategy.big-bang.name: Big Bang
dv.strategy.rolling.name: Rolling
dv.strategy.blue-green.name: Blue-Green
dv.strategy.canary.name: Canary
dv.strategy.feature-toggle.name: Feature Toggle
dv.strategy.shadow.name: Shadow
dv.properties.title: Strategy Properties
dv.svc.db.name: PostgreSQL
dv.svc.cache.name: Redis
dv.svc.queue.name: Kafka
dv.svc.search.name: Elasticsearch
dv.ui.outage: Service outage — users cannot be served
dv.log.step_mode: Step mode — use the ▶ / ◀ buttons to walk through
dv.tooltip.svc-db: Relational database (PostgreSQL or MySQL). During a deploy, watch the schema version, migration locks, and the fact that both app versions read and write it at once.
dv.tooltip.svc-cache: In-memory cache (Redis or Memcached). A schema change makes old cache entries stale — version the key namespace or flush, and expect a cold-cache period after the deploy.
dv.tooltip.svc-queue: Message broker (Kafka or RabbitMQ). During a rollout old and new consumers share one consumer group, so message payloads must stay backward-compatible.
dv.tooltip.svc-search: Search engine (Elasticsearch). Field mappings are mostly immutable — a mapping change needs a reindex into a new index, followed by an atomic alias swap.
dv.tooltip.flag: A feature flag gates the new code path. Turning it on or off is a configuration change — no redeploy — which makes it the fastest rollback available.
dv.tooltip.health-healthy: Instance is up and passing its readiness probe — it receives traffic.
dv.tooltip.health-starting: Instance is booting; the readiness probe is not green yet — no traffic.
dv.tooltip.health-unhealthy: Instance is failing its health check — removed from the load balancer.
dv.tooltip.health-draining: Instance is finishing in-flight requests before shutdown — no new traffic.
dv.tooltip.health-idle: Instance slot exists but is not running this version.
dv.tooltip.health-stopped: Instance is stopped or not deployed.
dv.big-bang.mode.success.name: Successful deploy
dv.big-bang.mode.migration-fail.name: Migration failure
dv.big-bang.mode.app-fail.name: Application failure
dv.big-bang.mode.success.desc: The simplest strategy: stop everything, migrate, deploy v2, start everything. One downtime window and no version overlap — but every user is offline while it runs.
dv.big-bang.mode.migration-fail.desc: The migration aborts halfway through the downtime window, leaving the schema inconsistent. Recovery means a database restore and a much longer outage.
dv.big-bang.mode.app-fail.desc: v2 fails its health check after a destructive migration already dropped the old columns — so rolling back to v1 is impossible without first restoring the database.
dv.big-bang.prop.downtime: Full outage for the entire deploy window (~30s and up)
dv.big-bang.prop.rollback: Slow — needs a redeploy, plus a DB restore if the migration was destructive
dv.big-bang.prop.cost: Cheap — one environment, no spare servers
dv.big-bang.prop.risk: High — every user is exposed at once, with no gradual check
dv.big-bang.prop.migration: A destructive migration is safe inside the downtime window
dv.big-bang.principles: Replace the whole fleet at once — no overlap between the old and new versions.
Accept a planned downtime window in exchange for a simple, predictable process.
Because no app runs during the window, the migration may be destructive in one step.
Always take a database backup before starting — it is the only recovery path.
Best scheduled for low-traffic hours and announced to users in advance.

dv.big-bang.concepts.definition: A planned interval where the service is fully offline while the deploy runs.
The Kubernetes name for the same idea: terminate all old pods, then create the new ones.
A schema change that drops or renames columns. Safe here only because no old code is running.
Rolling the database back to a pre-deploy snapshot — the recovery path when a migration fails.

dv.big-bang.pros: Simplest possible model — easy to understand and script.
No version overlap, so no backward-compatibility constraints on the migration.
Cheap — needs only one environment and no spare capacity.
Deterministic: the system is either fully old or fully new.
dv.big-bang.cons: Guaranteed downtime for every user during the deploy.
Rollback is slow and may require a database restore.
No gradual verification — problems hit 100% of users instantly.
A failed migration turns a short window into a long outage.
dv.big-bang.whenToUse: Use for internal tools, low-traffic apps, or systems with an agreed maintenance window — where simplicity matters more than availability.
dv.rolling.mode.success.name: Successful deploy
dv.rolling.mode.migration-fail.name: Migration failure
dv.rolling.mode.app-fail.name: Application failure
dv.rolling.mode.success.desc: Instances are replaced batch by batch behind the load balancer. Old and new versions run side by side, so the migration must be backward-compatible — expand now, contract later. Zero downtime.
dv.rolling.mode.migration-fail.desc: A destructive migration is run while v1 instances are still live, and they break instantly. The classic lesson in why rolling deploys require expand-only migrations.
dv.rolling.mode.app-fail.desc: A new batch fails its readiness probe. The orchestrator halts the rollout automatically and only the bad batch is rolled back — the cluster degrades but never goes down.
dv.rolling.prop.downtime: Zero — the load balancer always has healthy instances
dv.rolling.prop.rollback: Medium — roll back batch by batch, the same way it rolled out
dv.rolling.prop.cost: Cheap — reuses the same fleet plus a few spare slots
dv.rolling.prop.risk: Medium — old and new versions run at the same time
dv.rolling.principles: Replace instances in small batches; the load balancer always keeps healthy ones serving.
Old and new versions run together, so v2 must be compatible with v1's data and messages.
Run the expand migration before the rollout and the contract migration only after it.
A readiness probe gates each new instance — unhealthy ones never receive traffic.
Drain in-flight requests from an instance before stopping it.

dv.rolling.concepts.definition: Split a schema change into an additive 'expand' step (before deploy) and a destructive 'contract' step (after deploy).
A health check that decides whether an instance is ready to receive traffic.
Letting an instance finish its in-flight requests before it is shut down.
The period during a rollout when v1 and v2 both serve users and share the same data stores.

dv.rolling.pros: Zero downtime — capacity stays available throughout.
No extra environment needed; reuses the existing fleet.
A failed batch halts the rollout automatically.
Resource-efficient and well supported by orchestrators.
dv.rolling.cons: Old and new versions must be mutually compatible — extra discipline.
Migrations must be expand-only; a destructive one breaks live instances.
The mixed-version window can expose subtle data bugs.
dv.rolling.whenToUse: The default for most stateless services on Kubernetes or an autoscaling group — when you want zero downtime without paying for a second environment.
dv.blue-green.mode.success.name: Successful deploy
dv.blue-green.mode.migration-fail.name: Migration failure
dv.blue-green.mode.app-fail.name: Application failure
dv.blue-green.mode.success.desc: A full second environment (Green) is deployed and smoke-tested while Blue serves all traffic. The router then switches atomically, and Blue stays available as an instant rollback.
dv.blue-green.mode.migration-fail.desc: The migration on the shared database fails before the switch. Green cannot be tested, but Blue never stopped serving — zero user impact.
dv.blue-green.mode.app-fail.desc: Green fails its smoke tests, so the router is never flipped. No real user ever touched v2; the broken environment is simply torn down.
dv.blue-green.prop.downtime: Zero — an atomic router switch between environments
dv.blue-green.prop.rollback: Instant — flip the router back to Blue
dv.blue-green.prop.cost: High — needs a full duplicate environment
dv.blue-green.prop.risk: Low — v2 is verified before any traffic moves
dv.blue-green.prop.migration: Expand-only on the shared database; contract afterwards
dv.blue-green.principles: Run two identical environments — Blue is live, Green is idle.
Deploy and fully test v2 in Green while Blue serves every user.
Switch all traffic in one atomic router change once Green is verified.
Keep Blue running for a while — it is your instant rollback target.
Both environments share the database, so the migration must still be expand-only.

dv.blue-green.concepts.definition: Two production-grade environments; one serves live traffic while the other is prepared.
Moving 100% of traffic from Blue to Green in a single router or DNS change.
A focused set of real requests run against Green before it is made live.
Reverting by flipping the router back to the still-running Blue environment.

dv.blue-green.pros: Zero downtime and a truly instant rollback.
v2 is fully tested in a real environment before it serves anyone.
The switch is atomic — no mixed-version window for the app layer.
Failures before the switch have zero user impact.
dv.blue-green.cons: Doubles the infrastructure cost during the deploy.
The shared database still ties the two environments together.
Long-lived connections and in-flight work need careful handling at the switch.
Stateful components do not duplicate as cleanly as stateless ones.
dv.blue-green.whenToUse: Use when instant rollback and pre-flight verification are worth the cost of a second environment — common for critical user-facing services.
dv.canary.mode.success.name: Successful deploy
dv.canary.mode.migration-fail.name: Migration failure
dv.canary.mode.app-fail.name: Application failure
dv.canary.mode.success.desc: v2 is rolled out to a tiny slice of traffic first (5%), watched, then progressively widened to 100%. The blast radius of any problem is capped at the canary percentage.
dv.canary.mode.migration-fail.desc: A destructive migration on the shared database breaks the stable pool too — proof that a canary limits the blast radius of bad code, not of a bad migration.
dv.canary.mode.app-fail.desc: The canary's error rate spikes at 5% traffic. Automated rollback shifts everyone back to the stable pool — only a small slice of users was ever affected.
dv.canary.prop.downtime: Zero — the stable pool serves throughout
dv.canary.prop.rollback: Fast — shift traffic back to the stable pool
dv.canary.prop.cost: Low — one extra instance to start
dv.canary.prop.risk: Lowest — the blast radius is capped at the canary percentage
dv.canary.prop.migration: Expand-only on the shared database; contract afterwards
dv.canary.principles: Send a small percentage of traffic to v2 first; keep the rest on the stable pool.
Watch real metrics — error rate, latency — before widening the rollout.
Increase the canary percentage in steps, all the way to 100%.
Roll back by shifting traffic away — automatically when metrics breach a threshold.
The shared database still requires an expand-only migration.

dv.canary.concepts.definition: A small v2 deployment that takes a fraction of traffic so problems surface on few users.
The share of users a bad release can affect — kept small by the canary percentage.
Increasing traffic to a new version in stages, gated by metrics at each step.
A rule that pulls traffic off the canary when its metrics breach a threshold.

dv.canary.pros: Smallest blast radius of any rollout strategy.
Real production metrics drive the decision to proceed.
Rollback is fast — just shift traffic back.
Cheap to start — only one extra instance.
dv.canary.cons: Needs solid metrics and alerting to be effective.
The rollout takes longer than an all-at-once switch.
Old and new versions run together — compatibility still matters.
Does not protect against a bad shared-database migration.
dv.canary.whenToUse: Use for high-traffic services where you want to catch regressions on real users while limiting how many of them are exposed.
dv.feature-toggle.mode.success.name: Successful deploy
dv.feature-toggle.mode.app-fail.name: Application failure
dv.feature-toggle.mode.success.desc: v2 ships to the whole fleet with the new feature behind an OFF flag — deploy is fully decoupled from release. The feature is then ramped on by flag percentage.
dv.feature-toggle.mode.migration-fail.de: The migration the feature depends on fails before deploy. The flag stays OFF, v2 is never shipped, and users never see the feature.
dv.feature-toggle.mode.app-fail.desc: The new feature errors after the flag is turned on. Flipping the flag back OFF is an instant, redeploy-free rollback — the fastest there is.
dv.feature-toggle.prop.downtime: Zero — the code is already deployed and healthy
dv.feature-toggle.prop.rollback: Instant — flip the flag off, with no redeploy
dv.feature-toggle.prop.cost: Cheap — one environment, no spare fleet
dv.feature-toggle.prop.risk: Low — gradual exposure plus an instant kill switch
dv.feature-toggle.prop.migration: Expand-only; the flag gates the feature until it is ready
dv.feature-toggle.principles: Ship the new code to everyone, but keep the feature behind an OFF flag.
Deploying code and releasing a feature become two separate, independent decisions.
Ramp the flag up by percentage and watch metrics, just like a canary.
Turning the flag off is a config change — instant, with no redeploy.
The migration still runs first; the flag gates the feature until the schema is ready.

dv.feature-toggle.concepts.definition: A runtime switch that turns a code path on or off without deploying anything.
Deploy puts code on servers; release exposes it to users. A flag separates the two.
Shipping a feature in the OFF state so it is in production but invisible.
Turning a flag off to instantly disable a misbehaving feature.

dv.feature-toggle.pros: The fastest rollback possible — a config change, not a redeploy.
Deploy and release are decoupled, reducing release-day risk.
Enables gradual rollout, A/B tests, and per-user targeting.
dv.feature-toggle.cons: Flags accumulate as technical debt if never removed.
Many flags multiply the number of code paths to test.
A flag system itself must be reliable and well governed.
Does not remove the need for safe, expand-only migrations.
dv.feature-toggle.whenToUse: Use when you want to separate deploying code from releasing features — for trunk-based development, gradual rollouts, and experiments.
dv.shadow.mode.success.name: Successful deploy
dv.shadow.mode.migration-fail.name: Migration failure
dv.shadow.mode.app-fail.name: Application failure
dv.shadow.mode.success.desc: v2 runs as a shadow fleet that receives a mirrored copy of real production traffic. Its responses are discarded; it is validated under real load with zero user risk.
dv.shadow.mode.migration-fail.desc: The migration runs on an isolated database clone. Its failure stays entirely inside the shadow environment — production is never involved.
dv.shadow.mode.app-fail.desc: v2 errors on mirrored traffic. Because users only ever saw v1's responses, the bug is caught with zero production impact — before any real rollout.
dv.shadow.prop.downtime: Zero — the shadow fleet never serves real users
dv.shadow.prop.rollback: Trivial — nothing to roll back, traffic never moved
dv.shadow.prop.cost: High — an extra fleet plus cloned data stores
dv.shadow.prop.risk: Lowest — users are never exposed to v2 at all
dv.shadow.prop.migration: Run on an isolated database clone; production is untouched
dv.shadow.principles: Send v2 a mirrored copy of real production traffic — and discard its responses.
Users always get v1's response; v2 is only being measured.
Isolate v2's writes: a cloned database, a separate cache namespace, no real side effects.
Compare v2 against v1 on the same requests — latency, errors, response diffs.
Shadow is a validation step, not a release — promote with another strategy afterwards.

dv.shadow.concepts.definition: Duplicating live requests and sending the copy to a non-serving fleet.
Throwing away the shadow fleet's responses so users only ever see v1.
Routing v2's writes, emails, and payments to sandboxes so production stays clean.
Exercising v2 with the exact shape and volume of real traffic.

dv.shadow.pros: Tests v2 against real traffic with zero user risk.
Surfaces bugs that only real-world request shapes trigger.
Nothing to roll back — traffic never actually moved.
Great for validating performance and capacity before a release.
dv.shadow.cons: Expensive — a full extra fleet plus cloned data stores.
Side effects must be carefully isolated or they leak into production.
It validates v2 but does not release it — another strategy still must.
Mirroring infrastructure adds real operational complexity.
dv.shadow.whenToUse: Use to de-risk a major rewrite or a performance-critical change — validate it against real traffic before committing to a real rollout.
dv.s.big-bang.success.0: CI builds the v2 artifact and runs the full test suite.
dv.s.big-bang.success.1: Stop the entire v1 fleet at once — the application goes offline, downtime begins.
dv.s.big-bang.success.2: Run the migration inside the downtime window — with no app running, a destructive change is safe.
dv.s.big-bang.success.3: Migration applied: columns renamed and old ones dropped in a single irreversible step.
dv.s.big-bang.success.4: Deploy the v2 build to every server simultaneously.
dv.s.big-bang.success.5: Flush Redis — v1-shaped keys are stale against the new schema, so the cache starts cold.
dv.s.big-bang.success.6: Start an Elasticsearch reindex into idx_v2 because the field mapping changed.
dv.s.big-bang.success.7: Readiness probes run against the freshly started v2 fleet.
dv.s.big-bang.success.8: All v2 instances pass the probe; the reindex finishes and the alias points to idx_v2.
dv.s.big-bang.success.9: Bring the fleet online — downtime ends and every user is now served by v2.
dv.s.big-bang.success.10: v2 consumers drain the 14 Kafka messages that piled up during the outage.
dv.s.big-bang.success.11: First requests hit a cold cache — early users get cache misses and slower responses.
dv.s.big-bang.success.12: Cache is warm again; PostgreSQL serves v2 reads and writes normally.
dv.s.big-bang.success.13: Deployment complete. Big Bang is simple, but it cost ~31s of total downtime.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.0: CI builds the v2 artifact and runs the test suite.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.1: Stop the whole v1 fleet — the application goes offline.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.2: Run the schema migration inside the downtime window.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.3: The migration aborts halfway on a constraint violation — the schema is now half-applied.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.4: Neither version can boot: v2 needs the new schema, v1 needs the old one, the DB matches neither.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.5: Restore PostgreSQL from the pre-deploy backup — the only safe way out of a partial migration.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.6: Backup restored: the schema is back to a consistent v1 state.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.7: Redeploy the old v1 build that matches the restored schema.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.8: v1 is healthy again — but only after ~90s of outage, far longer than a normal release.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.9: Backlog drained, service restored to v1. The deploy never shipped.
dv.s.big-bang.migration-fail.10: Lesson: a Big Bang migration failure means an extended outage and a full database restore.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.0: CI builds the v2 artifact; tests are green.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.1: Stop the entire v1 fleet — application offline.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.2: Run a destructive migration — old columns are dropped because no app needs them now.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.3: Migration applied. The old v1 columns no longer exist in the database.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.4: Deploy v2 everywhere and flush the cache.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.5: Readiness probes start checking the v2 fleet.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.6: v2 fails the probe — a bad config value makes the application crash on boot.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.7: The obvious fix is to roll back to v1 — but v1's code expects the columns that were just dropped.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.8: Redeploy v1 anyway and hope it survives the v2 schema.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.9: v1 crashes too: its queries reference dropped columns. The destructive migration trapped you.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.10: Restore the database from backup so the old v1 code has its schema back.
dv.s.big-bang.app-fail.11: v1 recovers after a DB restore. Lesson: a destructive Big Bang migration makes rollback impossible without it.
dv.s.rolling.success.0: CI builds v2 and runs the test suite.
dv.s.rolling.success.1: Run the expand migration first: add new nullable columns. It is backward-compatible — v1 ignores them.
dv.s.rolling.success.2: Start the rolling deploy — replace instances one batch at a time, never all at once.
dv.s.rolling.success.3: Take batch 1 out of the load balancer, drain its in-flight requests, and start it on v2.
dv.s.rolling.success.4: Batch 1 passes readiness and rejoins the pool — now v1 and v2 serve users side by side.
dv.s.rolling.success.5: Both versions read and write PostgreSQL at once — exactly why the migration had to be additive.
dv.s.rolling.success.6: The Kafka consumer group now mixes v1 and v2 consumers — message payloads must stay compatible.
dv.s.rolling.success.7: v2 writes version-namespaced cache keys so it never reads v1-shaped data, and vice versa.
dv.s.rolling.success.8: v2 dual-writes documents to Elasticsearch while a background reindex builds idx_v2.
dv.s.rolling.success.9: Replace batch 2 the same way — drain, restart on v2, wait for readiness.
dv.s.rolling.success.10: Batch 2 is healthy — the whole fleet now runs v2 with zero downtime at any point.
dv.s.rolling.success.11: All consumers are v2, the search alias swaps to idx_v2, and the cache settles on v2 keys.
dv.s.rolling.success.12: No v1 code is left — run the contract migration to drop the old columns and finish the change.
dv.s.rolling.success.13: Rolling deploy complete — zero downtime, gradual, and no extra servers needed.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.1: Mistake: instead of an additive expand migration, a destructive RENAME is run while v1 is still live.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.2: The migration succeeds — but the columns the running v1 code reads no longer exist.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.3: Every live v1 instance breaks instantly — its SQL references missing columns. v2 is not even deployed yet.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.4: Users get 500s across the whole site — the destructive migration took down the live fleet.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.5: Restore PostgreSQL from backup to bring the v1 schema back.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.6: Schema restored to v1; the crashed instances restart.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.7: v1 recovers — but only after a partial outage and thousands of failed requests.
dv.s.rolling.migration-fail.8: Lesson: rolling deploys run old and new code together — migrations must be expand-only. A destructive change breaks the live fleet.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.1: Run the additive expand migration — backward-compatible, so the half-deployed state stays safe.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.2: Begin the rolling deploy, batch by batch.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.3: Drain batch 1 and start it on v2.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.4: The new v2 instances fail readiness — a missing env var. They never enter the load balancer.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.5: The orchestrator halts the rollout automatically: it will not drain more v1 while the new batch is unhealthy.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.6: No user ever reached v2 — unhealthy instances get no traffic — but capacity is down 50%.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.7: Roll back only the failed batch — redeploy v1 onto those two instances.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.8: All four instances are healthy v1 again — full capacity restored, no data lost.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.9: The expand columns stay in place — nullable and harmless — ready for the next attempt.
dv.s.rolling.app-fail.10: Lesson: rolling deploys fail safely — a bad batch halts the rollout and only that batch is rolled back.
dv.s.blue-green.success.0: CI builds v2 and runs the test suite.
dv.s.blue-green.success.1: Run the expand migration on the shared database — both Blue and Green will use this schema.
dv.s.blue-green.success.2: Deploy v2 to the idle Green environment. Blue keeps serving 100% of users.
dv.s.blue-green.success.3: Green boots and passes its internal health checks — still receiving no user traffic.
dv.s.blue-green.success.4: Run smoke tests against Green: real requests hit the v2 fleet directly.
dv.s.blue-green.success.5: Green's smoke tests read and write the shared PostgreSQL — safe, the schema fits both versions.
dv.s.blue-green.success.6: Green uses v2-namespaced cache keys, so its traffic never pollutes Blue's cached data.
dv.s.blue-green.success.7: A separate idx_v2 index is reindexed and kept ready; the live alias still points at idx_v1.
dv.s.blue-green.success.8: Smoke tests pass — Green is verified and ready to take production traffic.
dv.s.blue-green.success.9: Flip the router from Blue to Green in one atomic switch — every user moves to v2 instantly.
dv.s.blue-green.success.10: Swap the search alias to idx_v2 and cut the Kafka consumers over to v2.
dv.s.blue-green.success.11: Verify Green in production. Blue stays untouched — it is your instant rollback for a few minutes.
dv.s.blue-green.success.12: Once Green is trusted, retire Blue and run the contract migration to drop the old columns.
dv.s.blue-green.success.13: Blue-Green complete — zero downtime and an instant, atomic switch, at the cost of a second fleet.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.1: Run the expand migration on the shared database before touching Green.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.2: The migration fails — a long-held row lock times out and the statement aborts.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.3: Green cannot be deployed: its v2 code needs the new columns. The pipeline stops at the migration.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.4: Crucially, Blue was never touched — it kept serving 100% of users. Zero customer impact.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.5: The migration rolled itself back in its own transaction — the schema is still a consistent v1.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.6: Fix the migration (split it, build the index CONCURRENTLY) and retry — Blue runs the whole time.
dv.s.blue-green.migration-fail.7: Lesson: with Blue-Green a migration failure before the switch is a non-event — Blue absorbs everything.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.1: Run the additive expand migration on the shared database — it succeeds.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.2: Deploy v2 to Green. Blue still serves every user.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.3: Green's processes start and the basic health checks pass.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.4: Run the smoke-test suite against Green before flipping any traffic.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.5: Smoke tests fail — v2 returns wrong order totals at checkout. A logic bug slipped through.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.6: The router was never flipped — not one real user reached Green. Blue is still serving 100%.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.7: Tear down the broken Green environment. No live rollback needed — Blue never moved.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.8: The expand columns stay in the shared database — nullable and harmless — ready for the fixed v2.
dv.s.blue-green.app-fail.9: Lesson: Blue-Green catches a bad release in Green, before the switch, with zero user impact.
dv.s.canary.success.0: CI builds v2 and runs the test suite.
dv.s.canary.success.1: Run the backward-compatible expand migration — stable and canary will share this schema.
dv.s.canary.success.2: Deploy a single canary instance running v2 next to the stable pool.
dv.s.canary.success.3: The canary passes its readiness probe and joins the load balancer.
dv.s.canary.success.4: Route just 5% of users to the canary — the blast radius is capped at 5%.
dv.s.canary.success.5: The canary dual-writes — it fills both old and new columns so stable instances stay correct.
dv.s.canary.success.6: The canary uses v2-namespaced cache keys — cold misses for now, with no clash against stable.
dv.s.canary.success.7: The Kafka consumer group now has one v2 consumer — message payloads must stay compatible.
dv.s.canary.success.8: v2 dual-writes documents to Elasticsearch; searches are still served by the idx_v1 alias.
dv.s.canary.success.9: Watch the canary's error rate and p99 latency for several minutes — the metrics look healthy.
dv.s.canary.success.10: Metrics are good — widen the canary to 25% of traffic.
dv.s.canary.success.11: Still healthy — scale the canary pool up and shift to a 50 / 50 split.
dv.s.canary.success.12: All metrics green — promote v2 to 100% and retire the stable v1 pool.
dv.s.canary.success.13: No v1 code is left — run the contract migration to drop the old columns.
dv.s.canary.success.14: Canary complete — zero downtime, and never more than 5% of users exposed to an untested change.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.1: A destructive migration is run on the shared database — a mistake instead of an additive one.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.2: The old columns are gone — and the stable v1 pool reads this exact same database.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.3: All four stable v1 instances break instantly — and the canary was not even deployed yet.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.4: 100% of users get errors. A canary cannot contain a destructive migration — the DB is shared by all.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.5: Restore PostgreSQL from the pre-deploy backup.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.6: Schema restored to v1; the stable pool restarts.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.7: The stable pool recovers — but only after a full-site outage.
dv.s.canary.migration-fail.8: Lesson: Canary limits the blast radius of bad code, not of a bad shared-DB migration. Migrations must still be expand-only.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.1: Run the additive expand migration — it succeeds and is safe for both versions.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.2: Deploy one canary instance running v2.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.3: The canary passes its readiness probe and joins the pool.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.4: Route 5% of users to the canary and start measuring.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.5: Watch the canary's error rate and latency against the stable baseline.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.6: The canary's error rate spikes to 30% — a null-pointer bug on a path the tests missed.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.7: Only the 5% of users on the canary are affected — the other 95% on stable v1 see nothing wrong.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.8: Automated rollback fires: the router pulls all traffic back to the stable v1 pool.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.9: Remove the failed canary instance — stable v1 serves 100% again, seconds after detection.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.10: The expand columns stay in place — harmless and ready for a fixed v2.
dv.s.canary.app-fail.11: Lesson: Canary caught the bug with only ~5% of users briefly hit — the smallest blast radius of any rollout.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.0: CI builds v2 — the new feature is included but wrapped in an OFF feature flag.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.1: Run the expand migration: the feature's columns are added, nullable and unused for now.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.2: Deploy v2 to the whole fleet. With the flag OFF, v2 behaves exactly like v1.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.3: The fleet is healthy on v2 — but users notice nothing, the feature is still dark.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.4: Deploy is done; release has not started. The flag decouples shipping code from exposing it.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.5: Turn the flag ON for 1% of users — the first real exposure of the new feature.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.6: Flagged users exercise the new code path, which writes the new columns.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.7: The new path uses its own cache keys, so the 99% of flag-off users are unaffected.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.8: Watch metrics for the 1% — error rate and latency look fine.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.9: Ramp the flag to 10%. The new path emits new Kafka events, which consumers already understand.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.10: Ramp to 50% — the feature writes searchable documents into Elasticsearch.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.11: Ramp the flag to 100% — the feature is fully released, with no redeploy at any point.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.12: After the flag has held at 100% and proven stable, run the contract migration.
dv.s.feature-toggle.success.13: Feature Toggle complete — deploy and release stayed separate, and the rollout used zero redeploys.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.0: CI builds v2 with the new feature behind an OFF flag.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.1: Run the expand migration that the new feature depends on.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.2: The migration fails — the database volume runs out of disk space mid-statement.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.3: The pipeline stops here. The flag is still OFF and v2 was never shipped to the fleet.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.4: Users are entirely unaffected — they run v1, and the feature does not exist for anyone.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.5: The migration rolled back inside its transaction — the schema is a clean v1.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.6: Free up disk, fix the migration, retry. Even after a retry, the feature stays OFF until you choose.
dv.s.feature-toggle.migration-fail.7: Lesson: with the migration gated before deploy and the flag OFF, a migration failure exposes nothing.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.0: CI builds v2 with the new feature behind an OFF flag.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.1: Run the additive expand migration — it succeeds.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.2: Deploy v2 to the whole fleet with the flag OFF.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.3: v2 is deployed and healthy — the feature is still dark for everyone.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.4: Turn the flag ON for 10% of users.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.5: Within minutes the new code path throws errors for flagged users — an unhandled edge case.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.6: Only the 10% with the flag on are hit — and the app itself is fine, just the new path is broken.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.7: Flip the flag OFF. No deploy, no restart — the fix takes effect within seconds.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.8: Errors stop immediately — every user is back on the safe v1 code path, while v2 stays deployed.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.9: The expand columns and the deployed v2 build all stay in place — only the flag changed.
dv.s.feature-toggle.app-fail.10: Lesson: a feature flag is the fastest rollback there is — a config change, not a redeploy.
dv.s.shadow.success.0: CI builds v2 so it can be tested against real production traffic — safely.
dv.s.shadow.success.1: Provision a shadow database (a clone) and run the v2 migration there — production's schema is untouched.
dv.s.shadow.success.2: Deploy v2 as a separate shadow fleet, wired to the cloned data stores — not to production's.
dv.s.shadow.success.3: The shadow fleet boots and passes health checks — it serves no real users.
dv.s.shadow.success.4: Start mirroring: the router sends every live request to v1 and a copy of it to the shadow fleet.
dv.s.shadow.success.5: Users only ever get v1's response — the shadow fleet computes its answer and then discards it.
dv.s.shadow.success.6: v2 reads production data read-only for realism — but its writes go to the DB clone, never to prod.
dv.s.shadow.success.7: The shadow fleet uses its own cache namespace so it cannot evict or poison production's cache.
dv.s.shadow.success.8: Shadow consumers read mirrored messages but never commit offsets or trigger real side effects.
dv.s.shadow.success.9: v2 indexes documents into a shadow Elasticsearch index — production search is unaffected.
dv.s.shadow.success.10: Compare v2 to v1 under real load — latency, error rate, response diffs. v2 holds up well.
dv.s.shadow.success.11: Shadow testing passed — promote v2 with a real strategy (Canary or Blue-Green) and tear down the shadow fleet.
dv.s.shadow.success.12: Shadow complete — v2 was validated against real production traffic with zero risk to users.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.0: CI builds v2 for a shadow test run.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.1: Run the v2 migration on the shadow database clone.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.2: The migration fails on the clone — the snapshot was stale and a constraint now conflicts.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.3: The shadow fleet cannot start — but this is the shadow environment. Production's database was never touched.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.4: Not a single user is affected — mirroring never even started.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.5: Simply discard the broken clone — there is nothing to restore, production was never involved.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.6: Take a fresh snapshot of production, fix the migration, and retry the shadow run.
dv.s.shadow.migration-fail.7: Lesson: Shadow runs in a fully isolated environment — even a failed migration there has zero production impact.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.0: CI builds v2 for a shadow test run.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.1: Run the v2 migration on the database clone — it succeeds.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.2: Deploy v2 as an isolated shadow fleet.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.3: The shadow fleet boots and passes its health checks.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.4: Start mirroring live production traffic to the shadow fleet.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.5: Compare v2 against v1 on the mirrored request stream.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.6: v2 throws exceptions on 8% of mirrored requests — a request shape production sees that tests missed.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.7: Those errors happened only on shadow traffic — users got v1's correct responses the whole time.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.8: Stop mirroring and tear down the shadow fleet — there is nothing to roll back on the live side.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.9: Fix the bug, rebuild, and run the shadow test again before attempting any real rollout.
dv.s.shadow.app-fail.10: Lesson: Shadow surfaces real-world bugs before users are ever exposed — the safest pre-release test.
